critique.summary: The article introduces tinygrad, a simple neural network framework, and promotes the tinybox, a powerful deep learning computer with various configurations and high performance metrics. It highlights tinygrad's features, the tinybox's hardware specifications, pricing, and availability, as well as the company's hiring and development plans.
mainPoints: tinygrad is a minimalistic neural network framework that simplifies complex networks into three operation types.
tinybox is a deep learning computer available in different models (red, green, blackwell, exabox) with varying GPU power, RAM, and bandwidth.
The tinybox is claimed to offer high performance at a competitive price, benchmarked against more expensive systems in MLPerf Training 4.0.
tinygrad supports both inference and training with autodiff, and is used in applications like openpilot for driving models.
tinygrad development is ongoing on GitHub and Discord, with plans to improve speed and stability.
tinybox is currently available for purchase with fixed configurations and wire transfer payment only.
critique.truthfulness: The technical descriptions of tinygrad and tinybox align with publicly available information on the tinygrad GitHub repository and tinybox product pages, which confirm the framework's simplicity and the hardware specifications of the tinybox models. The claim about MLPerf Training 4.0 benchmarking is plausible but lacks direct citation or third-party verification in the article. The use of tinygrad in openpilot is consistent with known open-source projects. However, some performance claims, such as achieving 'likely the best performance/$' and the exabox's 'exaFLOP' scale, should be independently verified as they are promotional and not supported by external benchmarks in the text. Overall, the article is mostly accurate but promotional and lacks multiple independent sources.
considerations: The article does not provide independent third-party benchmarks or reviews to substantiate performance and cost-effectiveness claims.
No mention of potential limitations or comparisons with other popular frameworks and hardware beyond brief MLPerf reference.
The pricing and availability details may change and should be verified directly from the vendor.
The article lacks discussion on software ecosystem maturity, community support, and compatibility with common ML tools beyond tinygrad.
No information on warranty, customer support, or long-term maintenance for tinybox buyers.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the Tinybox AI device and related hardware, with users debating its pricing, technical specifications, target market, and practicality. Some express skepticism about the value and performance claims, while others appreciate the concept of local AI models and offline hardware options.

comments.author: bastawhiz
comments.text:
There's no way the red v2 is doing anything with a 120b parameter model. I just finished building a dual a100 ai homelab (80gb vram combined with nvlink). Similar stats otherwise. 120b only fits with very heavy quantization, enough to make the model schizophrenic in my experience. And there's no room for kv, so you'll OOM around 4k of context.

I'm running a 70b model now that's okay, but it's still fairly tight. And I've got 16gb more vram then the red v2.

I'm also confused why this is 12U. My whole rig is 4u.

The green v2 has better GPUs. But for $65k, I'd expect a much better CPU and 256gb of RAM. It's not like a threadripper 7000 is going to break the bank.

I'm glad this exists but it's... honestly pretty perplexing
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, experience-based critique of the hardware's capability to handle a 120B parameter model, highlighting VRAM limitations and technical constraints.

comments.author: vessenes
comments.text:
The exabox is interesting. I wonder who the customer is; after watching the Vera Rubin launch, I cannot imagine deciding I wanted to compete with NVIDIA for hyperscale business right now. Maybe it’s aiming at a value-conscious buyer? Maybe it’s a sensible buy for a (relatively) cash-strapped ML startup; actually I just checked prices, and it looks like Vera Rubin costs half for a similar amount of GPU RAM. I’m certain that the interconnect will not be as good as NV’s.

I have no idea who would buy this. Maybe if you think Vera Rubin is three years out? But NV ships, man, they are shipping.
comments.analysis: Offers a market perspective and pricing comparison, questioning the target audience and competitiveness of the product relative to established players.

comments.author: mmoustafa
comments.text:
I would love to see real-life tokens/sec values advertised for one or various specific open source models.

I'm currently shopping for offline hardware and it is very hard to estimate the performance I will get before dropping $12K, and would love to have a baseline that I can at least always get e.g. 40 tok/s running GPT-OSS-120B using Ollama on Ubuntu out of the box.
comments.analysis: Highlights a practical concern about transparency in performance metrics, reflecting a common user need for better benchmarking data.

comments.author: ekropotin
comments.text:
IDK, I feel it’s quite overpriced, even with the current component prices.

I almost sure it’s possible to custom build a machine as powerful as their red v2 within 9k budget. And have a lot of fun along the way.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid point about pricing and the feasibility of building comparable hardware more affordably, encouraging cost-conscious evaluation.

comments.author: ivraatiems
comments.text:
There's some irony in the fact that this website reads as extremely NOT AI-generated, very human in the way it's designed and the tone of its writing.

Still, this is a great idea, and one I hope takes off. I think there's a good argument that the future of AI is in locally-trained models for everyone, rather than relying on a big company's own model.

One thought: The ability to conveniently get this onto a 240v circuit would be nice. Having to find two different 120v circuits to plug this into will be a pain for many folks.
comments.analysis: Provides an observational comment on the website's design and tone, supporting the idea of local AI models but without technical depth.

comments.author: ilaksh
comments.text:
I thought the most interesting thing about tinygrad was that theoretically you could render a model all the way into hardware similar to Taalas (tinygrad might be where Taalas got the idea for all I know).

I could swear I filed a GitHub issue asking about the plans for that but I don't see it. Anyway I think he mentioned it when explaining tinygrad at one point and I have wondered why that hasn't got more attention.

As far as boxes, I wish that there were more MI355X available for normal hourly rental. Or any.
comments.analysis: Offers an insightful perspective on the potential hardware integration of tinygrad, though somewhat speculative about future developments.

comments.author: comrade1234
comments.text:
Cool that you have a dual power supply model. It says rack mountable or free standing. Does that mean two form factors? $65K is more than we can afford right now but we are definitely eventually in the market for something we can run in our own colo.

It's funny though... we're using deepseek now for features in our service and based on our customer-type we thought that they would be completely against sending their data to a third-party. We thought we'd have to do everything locally. But they seem ok with deepseek which is practically free. And the few customers that still worry about privacy may not justify such a high price point.
comments.analysis: Shares user perspective on product features and pricing, reflecting real-world considerations for potential buyers.

comments.author: wongarsu
comments.text:
Sound like solid prebuilt with well balanced components and a pretty case

Not revolutionary in any way, but nice. Unless I'm missing something here?
comments.analysis: Gives a balanced, moderate view on the product's design and innovation, suggesting it is competent but not groundbreaking.

comments.author: aabaker99
comments.text:
> Can I pay with something besides wire transfer? In order to keep prices low and quality high, we don't offer any customization to the box or ordering process. Wire transfer is the only accepted form of payment.

Sorry, what? Is this just a scam?
comments.analysis: Expresses suspicion about the payment method and lack of customization, but the accusation of a scam is speculative and lacks evidence.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.text:
My interest in anything associated with geohot took a colossal nose dive today after seeing this post against democracy, quoting frelling M*ncius M*ldbug: Democracy is a Liability. https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47469543 https://geohot.github.io//blog/jekyll/update/2026/03/21/demo...

Theres a lot there that makes sense & I think needs to be considered. But a lot just seems to be out of the blue, included without connection, in my view. Feels like maybe are in-grouo messages, that I don't understand. How this is headered as against democracy is unclear to me, and revolting. I both think we must grapple with the world as it is, and this post is in that area, strongly, but to let fear be the dominant ruling emotion is one of the main definitions of conservativism, and it's use here to scare us sounds bad.
comments.analysis: This comment diverges into political opinion and personal reaction unrelated to the technical discussion, reducing its relevance and constructive value.
